draft.introduction: The chariot allegory at Phaedrus 246a–b is Plato’s most vivid image of the soul’s structure. The soul is likened to a charioteer driving two winged horses—one noble and one unruly—upward toward the vision of true Being.

translation.text: Let the soul be likened to the conjoined power of a team of winged horses and their charioteer. Now the horses and charioteers of the gods are all good and of good stock, but those of other beings are mixed. With us, the charioteer drives a pair; one horse is noble and good, the other the opposite.
translation.original_text: ἐοικέτω δὴ συμφύτῳ δυνάμει ὑποπτέρου ζεύγους τε καὶ ἡνιόχου

translator_notes.note: The chariot allegory maps onto Plato’s tripartite psychology from the Republic: the charioteer is reason, the noble horse is spiritedness, and the unruly horse is appetite. The image of upward flight toward Being became central to Neoplatonic accounts of the soul’s ascent.
